title: Estimating carcass yield from a home pig
subtitle: What liveweight becomes in the freezer after slaughter and cutting.
excerpt: A pig you've bought at 8 weeks and grown to slaughter weight doesn't end up entirely in the freezer. Gut fill, head, trotters, offal, trim, and bone all reduce the saleable yield. Knowing how the numbers work before the pig goes to the abattoir prevents disappointment and helps you plan the freezer space and the meat order.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, carcass yield and grading guidance. DEFRA/APHA pig movement and traceability regulations. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, pig chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The weight of the dressed carcass after slaughter, head removal, and evisceration, the starting point for calculating usable meat yield.
Deadweight as a percentage of liveweight; typically 73,78% in pigs, depending on breed and gut fill.

aliases: how much pork from one pig

content.text: A pig that leaves the holding at 85 kg liveweight produces a

of around 73,78%. From that deadweight, you'll get roughly 50,55 kg of packaged pork, once the butcher removes bone, excess fat, and trim. A 90 kg liveweight pig from a traditional breed like the Tamworth or Berkshire runs slightly higher on fat and slightly lower on lean yield per kg than a commercial cross.
Saleable packaged pork: around 50 kg (79% of deadweight)
Offal (liver, heart, kidneys, tongue, head meat): 3,5 kg if you take it
Bone, trim, skin: accounts for the remainder

Traditional breeds are often slaughtered at heavier weights (90,110 kg liveweight) to allow the fat to develop fully. Commercial crosses aimed at fresh pork are typically slaughtered at 80,95 kg. A lighter pig produces a higher proportion of bone to meat; a heavier pig produces more fat trim.

50 kg of packaged pork takes around 70,80 litres of freezer space. A 200-litre chest freezer handles one pig comfortably. Two pigs, or a large traditional-breed pig over 100 kg liveweight, needs more room or a second freezer organised in advance.

Most small abattoirs charge a per-head kill fee plus a per-kg cutting and packaging fee. Get the full quote before booking, abattoir costs on a small pig can be disproportionate to the yield if you use a facility that specialises in cattle. Some butchers who do home-kill services charge a flat fee per pig for the full slaughter-and-cut service.
